coredata.dc:description: A thermo-reversible electroconductive hydrogel composed of carbon dots (CDs)/TiO2 fluorinated silica facilitates solar-driven water purification through pore size modulation, governed by a transition between hydrophobic and hydrophilic states. This transition is monitored by changes in electrical resistance. The inclusion of the photocatalyst TiO2 in the CDs enhances hydrophilicity under visible light irradiation, enabling a reversible shift in surface affinity. This shift influences the volume transition of the CD-hydrogel within temperature ranges of 35 °C–55 °C and 0 °C–25 °C, corresponding to upper and lower critical solution temperatures, respectively. The hydrogel exhibits a temperature increase of 52.4 °C and a swelling ratio of 223 % upon solar light exposure. Water purification is achieved through water absorption and release cycles involving immersion and irradiation for 100 min, leading to a mass reduction of 2.435 kg/m². Moreover, bacterial contamination in water is purified and monitored through a measurable change in electrical resistance from 12.5 to 27.6 kΩ. These resistance variations enable real-time observation of water purification using electrochemical analysis and smartphone connectivity.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A thermo-reversible electroconductive hydrogel composed of carbon dots (CDs)/TiO2 fluorinated silica facilitates solar-driven water purification through pore size modulation, governed by a transition between hydrophobic and hydrophilic states. This transition is monitored by changes in electrical resistance. The inclusion of the photocatalyst TiO2 in the CDs enhances hydrophilicity under visible light irradiation, enabling a reversible shift in surface affinity. This shift influences the volume transition of the CD-hydrogel within temperature ranges of 35 °C–55 °C and 0 °C–25 °C, corresponding to upper and lower critical solution temperatures, respectively. The hydrogel exhibits a temperature increase of 52.4 °C and a swelling ratio of 223 % upon solar light exposure. Water purification is achieved through water absorption and release cycles involving immersion and irradiation for 100 min, leading to a mass reduction of 2.435 kg/m². Moreover, bacterial contamination in water is purified and monitored through a measurable change in electrical resistance from 12.5 to 27.6 kΩ. These resistance variations enable real-time observation of water purification using electrochemical analysis and smartphone connectivity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The natural water cycle operates through the evaporation of water bodies by sunlight and the absorption of water by plants from soil during transpiration [1,2]. The Earth's surface receives sunlight irradiation at approximately 750 W/m² daily [3]. Considering this intensity and the greenhouse effect, which elevates Earth's temperature, the potential for energy conversion from photons, particularly solar irradiation, to heat should be explored. Responsive hydrogels, which can extensively and reversibly alter their volume in response to temperature, pH, humidity, ionic strength, electric fields, and other biochemical signals, have gained significant attention for their diverse applications [4–7]. Among external stimuli-responsive hydrogels, those responsive to temperature are particularly promising due to their convenience and minimal reliance on the chemical environment, allowing greater adaptability under various conditions [8–11]. Thermo-responsive hydrogels exhibit volume transitions induced by phase changes in the polymer system at the lower critical solution temperature (LCST) or the upper critical solution temperature (UCST) [12–14].

Among various thermo-responsive polymers, poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAAm) is widely used as a hydrogel with distinct properties above its LCST (∼32.5 °C) [15,16]. Conversely, poly(N-acryloyl glycinamide) (PNAGA) is a thermo-reversible hydrogel with a temperature response in the low range of 0 °C–32.5 °C, representing the UCST of the polymer [17]. Combining PNIPAAm and PNAGA has proven to be a facile strategy for producing a double-network thermo-responsive hydrogel that achieves a broad and adjustable temperature sensitivity due to dual LCST-UCST behavior. These hydrogels also possess robust physico-mechanical properties, including high stretchability and swelling capability [18,19]. Compared to single-network hydrogels, the double-network PNIPAAm-PNAGA hydrogels exhibit excellent swelling-deswelling properties, crucial for determining water evaporation rates and purification efficiency [20–22]. Consequently, double-network thermo-responsive PNIPAAm-PNAGA hydrogels are widely employed for rapid and efficient hydrogel-based water purification. Despite the thermosensitive nature of these polymers, hydrogels seldom demonstrate the ability to absorb photons from sunlight and undergo volume transitions through photothermal effects. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) and polymer dots (PDs) have been recognized for their ability to absorb a broad light spectrum and convert photons into heat. These materials exhibit exceptional properties, including fluorescence, excellent biocompatibility, and functionalization ease [23–25]. CDs and PDs possess sp²-sp³ configurations, showcasing optoelectronic properties derived from the recombination of photoexcited electron-hole (e⁻/h⁺) pairs, facilitating an optimal energy band gap for visible light absorption [26–28]. Incorporating CDs or PDs, which convert light to heat (e.g., fluorinated carbonized polydopamine/f-cPDA), with titanium oxide (TiO₂) into a thermo-responsive hydrogel enables the absorption of sunlight across the UV, visible, and NIR spectra. This process facilitates specific properties, including hydrophobic-hydrophilic transitions that affect volume changes and photothermal generation under sunlight exposure [27,29].

In this study, we fabricate a carbon dots (CDs)/TiO2 fluorinated silica (f-cPDA/TiO2)-loaded electroconductive thermo-responsive hydrogel (CD-hydrogel) for monitoring water purification based on volume changes induced by solar light irradiation and temperature shifts. The CD-hydrogel is designed using the LCST of PNIPAAm, the UCST of PNAGA, and the photothermal conversion of dopamine-conjugated fluorinated silica (f-cPDA/TiO₂). Sunlight absorption and heat conversion by f-cPDA/TiO₂ induce volume transitions in the hydrogel through swelling and shrinking. At the UCST and LCST, the crosslinked hydrogel becomes compact and reverts to its initial state at room temperature. Solar light-driven volume transitions drive the purification of water contaminated with bacteria and other pollutants. Hypothetically, water is absorbed into the hydrogel matrix via swelling, while hydrophobic interactions between the embedded PDs and debris facilitate waste adsorption. Solar light irradiation increases the hydrogel's internal temperature through the photothermal conversion ability of polydopamine, which transforms light into heat and controls the dimensions of the thermo-responsive hydrogel. This dimensional shift expels water from the hydrogel matrix, resulting in purified water. Additionally, the volume transitions resulting from water absorption and evaporation can be monitored electrochemically by measuring electrical resistance changes during repeated cycles of water absorption and solar light irradiation.

Dopamine hydrochloride (DA), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), 1H,1H,2H,2H-perfluorodecyl triethoxysilane (fluorosilane), TiO2 (anatase nanopowder, < 25 nm particle size, surface area [45−55 m2/g]), glycinamide hydrochloride, acryloyl chloride, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH), N-Isopropylacrylamide (NIPAAm), Acrylamide (AAm), N,N′-Bis(acryloyl)cystamine (BIS), ammonium persulfate (APS), N′-Tetramethyl ethylenediamine (TEMED), potassium carbonate, potassium persulfate (KPS), and sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) were procured from Sigma-Aldrich and used without further purification. Diethyl ether, ethanol, and cyclohexane were purchased from Samchun Chemical Co. Ltd. The preparation of f-cPDA/TiO2 and N-acryloylglycinamide (monomer of NAGA) followed previously reported methods optimized for specific properties [17,29].

An Optizen 2020UV (Mecasys, South Korea) spectrometer has been utilized to record UV–visible (UV–vis) spectra, and a Zetasizer Nano (Malvern, Germany) analyzed particle size. A FluoroMate fluorometer (Scinco, Seoul, South Korea) collected photoluminescence (PL) spectra. A solar simulator (DPR500, output wavelength 350–1000 nm, DY Tech Co., South Korea) with a light intensity of 100 mW/cm2 was used to irradiate the hydrogel, mimicking sunlight (1 sun). SEM images were captured using a JSM-6700F scanning electron microscope (JEOL, Musashino, Akishima, Tokyo, Japan). A HAAKE MARS modular advanced rheometer with 40 mm parallel-plate geometry measured rheology. A texture analyzer (SurTA 1Ae system, Chemilab Co., South Korea) evaluated tensile mechanics. Static water contact angles were measured using a DO3210 (KRUSS DSA 100 Optima system, Germany). Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) investigations were conducted with a DSC2910 (DSCV4.4E, TA Instrument, USA). Fluorescence images were captured using a JSM-6700F microscope (JEOL, Musashino, Akishima, Tokyo, Japan). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed using a Corrtest equipment (Wuhan, China). A Keithley 2450 Sourcemeter (Tektronix, Inc., USA) assessed pressure-sensing performance. Confocal Laser Scanning Images were acquired with the ECLIPSE Ti2-E confocal microscope (Nikon, Japan), and flow cytometry data were collected using the Attune NxT Acoustic Focused Cytometer (Life Technologies). The hydrogel's real-time sensing capability was evaluated using a custom wireless device comprising an Arduino Uno microcontroller (ATmega328P Processor), a self-designed circuit, a smartphone, and Bluetooth modules (AppGosu).

The PNIPAAm hydrogel was synthesized as follows: NIPAAm (1.5 g) and BIS (0.6 mg) were dissolved in double-distilled water (DDW), sonicated for 30 min until completely dissolved, and deoxygenated for 2 h in an N2 environment. APS (10 mg) and TEMED (10 μL) were added to the solution, which was polymerized for 24 h. The resulting PNIPAAm hydrogel was purified by dialysis against DDW (MWCO = 3.5 kDa) overnight and lyophilized. Subsequently, 1 g of PNIPAAm hydrogel, 25 mg of f-cPDA/TiO2, 0.5 g of NAGA, and 6 mg of BIS were dissolved in 10 mL of DDW at 4°C. After complete dispersion, KPS (50 mg) and TEMED (10 μL) were added, and the mixture was poured into a mold to polymerize for 24 h, yielding the CD-hydrogel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visible light treatment of the f-cPDA/TiO2 and CD-hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Visible light treatment was conducted using an LED lamp (OA-LED1 5 V, 650 mA) placed inside a light-isolated box lined with a black substrate to prevent external light penetration. For the f-cPDA/TiO2 nanoparticles, the samples were dispersed in micro-centrifuge tubes and irradiated within the chamber. The CD-hydrogel was placed in a Petri dish containing the treatment solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electronic resistivity, pressure sensing, wireless analysis of CD-hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The conductivity of CD-hydrogel samples (1 cm × 1 cm × 0.5 cm) was evaluated using the EIS technique with a Corrtest Electrochemical Workstation [30]. Measurements were performed using a two-electrode system, connecting the hydrogel edges to copper wire electrodes. EIS data were recorded over a frequency range of 0.1 Hz to 10 kHz at 0 V against the open-circuit potential (OCP). Direct current (DC) resistance was recorded with a Keithley 2450 SourceMeter at room temperature using a BIAS voltage of 1 V. Wireless electronic analysis involved attaching custom wireless devices to the hydrogel, with data transmitted to a smartphone via Bluetooth. Resistance values were captured in real-time and visualized as graphs [31,32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of water loss temperature treatment and solar light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para:
The change in mass after treatment was measured for water and CD-hydrogel following a 1-h water uptake. For temperature treatment, the hydrogel was placed in a Petri dish and exposed to a water bath at 45 °C and 4 °C, corresponding to LCST and UCST, respectively. For solar light irradiation, the CD-hydrogel was illuminated using a solar light apparatus at an intensity of 100 mW/cm². The mass change was calculated using the following equation:
Mass change (
kg
m
2
) =
∆ m
A
where Δm represents the change of mass during water loss, and A denotes the area of the fully swollen hydrogel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Purification of water from oil-water mixture using CD-hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Water purification utilizing the UCST and LCST properties of the hydrogel was performed as follows. To prepare an oil-in-water (O/W) emulsion, 5 % v/v oil was added to water containing 1 mg/mL SDS (final mass ratio is 3:100) and vortexed at 800 rpm for 1 h. The hydrogel, shaped into a cylinder (1.25 cm × 1 cm diameter and height), was submerged in the O/W mixture for 1 h to allow swelling. Excess surface water was wiped off with paper before transferring the swollen hydrogel to a Petri dish. The hydrogel was then partially immersed in a 45 °C water bath to prevent external water intrusion.

For river water purification, water samples from the Dalcheon River (Chungju, South Korea) were collected and treated similarly. Samples were collected at five distinct locations, with measurements repeated thrice for each site. The electrical resistance of the CD-hydrogel was monitored throughout the purification process, and the corresponding data were recorded in real time using a smartphone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and characterization of thermo-responsive electro-conductive CD-hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The CD-hydrogel was engineered using solar light-responsive f-cPDA/TiO2 for temperature-triggered volume shift water purification under solar light exposure. The PNIPAAm-PNAGA hydrogel matrix’s LCST and UCST characteristics, combined with the photocatalytic activity and photothermal conversion properties of f-cPDA/TiO2 played a critical role in regulating water absorption and desorption during temperature-triggered swelling and shrinking transitions [29,33,34]. The incorporation of TiO₂ was specifically designed to control the hydrophobic-hydrophilic transition of f-cPDA/TiO2 under sunlight, inducing volume change and enhancing the CD-hydrogel’s swelling capability and water purification rate. The sterilization efficiency was further improved through the photothermal conversion of f-cPDA/TiO2, particularly under NIR irradiation, where photothermolysis of bacteria occurred during wastewater purification. Compared to conventional purification methods such as activated carbon and membranes, this hydrogel system's innovative approach leverages solar light to regulate f-cPDA/TiO2 hydrophobicity and hydrophilicity as a result of tuning energy band gap and generating photocatalytic activity under UV and visible light irradiation. This system also demonstrates fluorescence on-off properties, high swelling-deswelling capability, and photothermal effects for bacterial ablation, making it highly efficient for water purification. Additionally, integrating CD-hydrogel with a wireless sensing device enables real-time monitoring of resistance changes, which are visualized on a smartphone to track purification efficiency.

Solar light-responsive nanoparticles were synthesized by conjugating dopamine to fluorinated silica nanoparticles, yielding hydrophobic nanoparticles (f-PDA). Carbonization of f-PDA produced silica-decorated polymer dots (f-cPDA), which were integrated with TiO2 to form f-cPDA/TiO2 (Figure S1a). UV–vis spectroscopy revealed catechol peaks for dopamine at 320 nm for f-cPDA/TiO2, indicating the retention of PDA after carbonization and TiO₂ integration (Figure S1b) [35]. Broad absorption peaks from 500 to 1000 nm indicated effective light absorption in the far-visible and NIR ranges [36]. This UV–vis-NIR spectra from 200 to 1000 nm demonstrated the as-synthesized f-cPDA/TiO2 nanoparticles’ capacity to absorb sunlight across UV (200–380 nm), visible (380–780 nm), and NIR light (>780 nm) wavelengths [37,38]. DLS measurements showed a reduction in the hydrodynamic diameter of f-cPDA/TiO2 from 249.1 to 174.4 nm after solar light irradiation, confirming the particle size decrease (Figure S1c). Photoluminescence spectroscopy further indicated low fluorescence due to aggregate-caused quenching (ACQ) and Förster resonance energy transfer, both of which increased upon solar light irradiation (100 mW/cm2), as shown in Figure S1d [39]. The nanoparticles’ photocatalytic activity was demonstrated using methylene blue (MB) degradation, with a decrease in UV–vis absorption peaks at 660–670 nm after 60 min of irradiation (Figure S1e) [40].

After evaluating the properties of the nanoparticles, a CD-hydrogel was fabricated by incorporating f-cPDA/TiO2 nanoparticles into the PNIPAAm-PNAGA hydrogel matrix. EDX spectroscopy with elemental mapping revealed the elemental composition of the CD-hydrogel, comprising C, N, O, and Ti, indicating a uniform distribution of f-cPDA/TiO2 nanoparticles within the hydrogel (Figure S2a-b). Confocal microscopy further demonstrated strong fluorescence emission in CD-hydrogel, particularly after solar light irradiation, confirming the successful integration of f-cPDA/TiO2 into the PNIPAAm-PNAGA hydrogel matrix (Figure S2c). The CD-hydrogel was utilized for water purification through swelling and temperature-induced shrinking ( Fig. 1a). Upon exposure to environmental temperature changes and solar irradiation, water was released while debris was trapped within the matrix due to intermolecular interactions with the embedded f-cPDA/TiO2. The temperature and irradiation-induced volume transitions were monitored electrochemically by measuring shifts in electrical resistance. The swelling capabilities of the f-cPDA/TiO2 nanoparticles were quantified and compared with a control PNIPAAm-PNAGA hydrogel (Figure S3). Swelling ratios of 223 % and 201.3 % were observed for the CD-hydrogel and the control hydrogel, respectively, after immersion for 240 h, with equilibrium reached within 120 min for both. The enhanced swelling of the CD-hydrogel was attributed to its softer structure and larger pore size compared to the PNIPAAm-PNAGA hydrogel without nanoparticles. Rheological analysis (Figure S4a) revealed that the storage modulus (G') and loss modulus (G'') of the CD-hydrogel were lower than those of the control, confirming a softer structure. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images also showed greater porosity in the CD-hydrogel, allowing higher water uptake (Figure S4b). The CD-hydrogel was subjected to varying environmental conditions, including a temperature increase to 45 °C (LCST) and a decrease to 4 °C (UCST). At equilibrium, the mass reduction was calculated to be 2.592 and 1.867 kg/m² for the LCST and UCST conditions, respectively (Fig. 1b). This mass reduction was associated with water loss during hydrogel shrinkage due to temperature-induced changes in the matrix. Mechanical properties were evaluated by measuring the compression performance at a strain of 50 %. At 25 °C, the stress was 19.41 kPa, which increased by 38.89 % and 38.11 % at 4 °C and 45 °C, respectively (Fig. 1c). Tensile testing revealed elongation at break values of 169 % and 162 % for the CD-hydrogel at 4 °C and 45 °C, respectively (Fig. 1d). These results indicate that temperature changes alter the molecular network of the hydrogel, enhancing its mechanical strength. [41–43]. The thermosensitive behavior of the CD-hydrogel was analyzed using a rheological temperature sweep (Fig. 1e). The storage modulus (G′) was lower than the loss modulus (G′') between 21 °C and 41 °C, indicating a gel-to-sol transition above the UCST (0 °C–20 °C) and a sol-to-gel transition above the LCST (>40 °C). Rheological reversibility was demonstrated over three cycles, with temperature adjustments between 25 °C–45 °C (LCST range) and 4 °C–25 °C (UCST range) (Fig. 1f and 1g). These results confirm the reversible nature of the hydrogel under varying thermal conditions. The temperature also influenced the photocatalytic activity of the CD-hydrogel. As shown in Figure S5, MB degradation was significantly higher at 25 °C compared to 4 °C and 45 °C, as indicated by lower MB absorbance at 660–670 nm. The enhanced photocatalytic performance at 25 °C was attributed to the higher swelling capability of the hydrogel at this temperature, which facilitated greater MB absorption and promoted photocatalytic degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of solar light on optical, electrical, and mechanical performances of CD-hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The static contact angle of the CD-hydrogel was measured under various conditions, including with and without solar light irradiation, at the LCST (45 °C) and UCST (4 °C). The initial contact angle of the hydrogel was 70°, which decreased to 55°, 58°, and 50° under the respective conditions, as shown in Fig. 2a and S6. The reduction in contact angle at the LCST and UCST was attributed to hydrogel matrix shrinkage and an increase in the water absorption rate. Solar light irradiation transformed the hydrogel surface from hydrophobic to hydrophilic due to the photocatalytic activity of TiO₂ [44]. Additionally, fluorescence images of the CD-hydrogel (Fig. 2b) revealed an increase in intensity following solar light irradiation, likely due to a reduction in the ACQ effect.

The electrical resistance of the CD-hydrogel was analyzed using electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (Fig. 2c). The hydrogel's impedance decreased from 23.49 kΩ to 18.98 kΩ after solar light irradiation at an intensity of 100 mW/cm². Wireless analysis results (Fig. 2d) corroborated this finding, showing reduced electronic signals after irradiation. The observed increase in electrical conductivity post-irradiation was attributed to changes in hydrophilicity, which influenced water content, electron mobility, and the hydrogel network [45]. Solar light irradiation enhanced water content, thereby promoting electron mobility through hydrogen bonding and improving the hydrogel's structural coordination. The network coordination of the CD-hydrogel was further investigated using differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) (Fig. 2e). Endothermic peaks at 97 °C, 129 °C, and 173 °C were associated with the melting points (Tm) of PNIPAAm, PNAGA, and the embedded CD, respectively [46,47]. These Tm values shifted after solar light irradiation, a phenomenon attributed to the hydrophobic-to-hydrophilic affinity transition triggered by TiO₂'s photocatalytic activity.

The morphological changes of the CD-hydrogel were examined using SEM images (Fig. 2f). Before irradiation, the hydrogel exhibited a larger pore size, correlating with greater swelling at room temperature. After solar light irradiation, the CD-hydrogel became compact, likely due to the reorganization of its network caused by temperature-induced particle size adjustments and matrix repositioning. To evaluate the advantages of the double-network matrix in the CD-hydrogel (containing PNIPAAm-PNAGA) for solar light-induced water purification, its performance was compared with a single-network hydrogel composed of PNIPAAm loaded with f-cPDA/TiO₂ (PNIPAAm-CD). The double-network CD-hydrogel demonstrated superior water purification efficiency and evaporation rate (1.591 kgm⁻²h⁻¹) compared to the single-network PNIPAAm-CD (0.998 kgm⁻²h⁻¹), as shown in Figures S7a and S7b. This improvement highlights the enhanced performance of the double-network design for water purification applications.

The effect of solar light irradiation on the swelling ratio and mass change of the CD-hydrogel was investigated, as illustrated in Fig. 3a and 3b. Without exposure to solar light (100 mW/cm²), the swelling ratio of the hydrogel was 190 % after 4 h of immersion. Upon irradiation, the water retention increased to 223 %, attributed to the hydrophobic-to-hydrophilic transition, which enhanced water absorption in the CD-hydrogel (Fig. 3a). This affinity shift allowed the hydrogel to absorb and maintain a higher water content compared to its hydrophobic state. The water loss due to irradiation was analyzed, showing a mass reduction to 2.435 kg/m² after 100 min of illumination (Fig. 3b). This behavior was tested by subjecting the hydrogel to solar light and capturing infrared images during the irradiation period. The irradiated CD-hydrogel reached a temperature of 52.4 °C, indicating significant photothermal conversion compared to the negligible temperature increase in the non-irradiated hydrogel (Fig. 3c). Strain sweep analysis of the CD-hydrogel with and without solar irradiation revealed changes in its storage modulus (Fig. 3d). Post-irradiation, the modulus increased from 1.82 to 207.5 Pa at 100 % strain, suggesting a transformation in the hydrogel's compactness and intermolecular network due to photocatalytic effects and temperature changes. The hydrogel exhibited reversible rheology after being subjected to alternating conditions (25 °C and solar light irradiation), as depicted in Fig. 3e. This reversibility was associated with increased temperature during irradiation, which affected the hydrogel network and enhanced its mechanical resistance to shearing deformation. The tensile properties of the CD-hydrogel were also evaluated (Fig. 3f). Before irradiation, the elongation at break was 108.8 %, which increased to 141.3 % after solar light exposure. This improvement was linked to the hydrophilic transition, which strengthened the intermolecular network through hydrogen bonding, enhancing the mechanical performance of the hydrogel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual thermo- and solar light-responsiveness of CD-hydrogel for water purification
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4a illustrates the dual responsiveness of the CD-hydrogel in temperature-dependent water absorption and discharge. Initially, the hydrogel was immersed in water for 1 h and placed in an empty Petri dish (step 1). The environmental temperature was adjusted to 45 °C (LCST) or 4 °C (UCST), triggering the release of absorbed water (steps 2 and 4). Subsequently, the hydrogel was immersed in water again to allow swelling (steps 3 and 5). Electrical resistance at each step was evaluated using a potentiostat (EIS), sourcemeter, and wireless analysis. The Nyquist plot (Fig. 4b) shows the hydrogel's response to external temperature changes corresponding to LCST and UCST. Shrinkage due to water release decreased resistance as the dimensions of the hydrogel matrix reduced (steps 2 and 4). Returning the hydrogel to room temperature increased its resistance as swelling restored the original dimensions (steps 3 and 5). Sourcemeter measurement recorded resistance changes from 48.7 kΩ (step 1) to 15.7 kΩ and 17.9 kΩ at LCST (step 2) and UCST (step 4), respectively, with reversions to 46.6 kΩ and 46.8 kΩ at steps 3 and 5 (Fig. 4c). Wireless analysis confirmed these trends, indicating higher resistance values at room temperature compared to LCST and UCST conditions. The CD-hydrogel underwent eight cycles of alternating heating and swelling, with electrical resistance quantified throughout (Figure S8). Conductivity increased under heat exposure due to the volume transition at LCST. The water loss caused a denser network, hindering electron movement and raising resistance [48]. During swelling, resistance decreased as water content increased, enhancing electron mobility. The hydrogel’s maximum water accumulation during swelling was observed to coincide with a small water release, maintaining higher conductance at this stage. To assess the impact of salinity on the hydrogel’s electroconductivity during water purification, experiments were conducted using saline water (1 % NaCl). Salt accumulation within the hydrogel during swelling and deswelling was indicated by a gradual decrease in resistance (Figure S9). This behavior resulted from interactions between Na⁺ ions in the saline water and carboxyl groups in f-cPDA/TiO₂, demonstrating the hydrogel’s potential application for saline wastewater desalination.

The solar light-responsive behavior of the hydrogel was further analyzed (Fig. 4d). The hydrogel was irradiated with solar light (100 mW/cm²) during even-numbered steps, and resistance was measured. Odd-numbered steps involved immersion in water, and resistance was estimated. Prolonged irradiation reduced the resistance to 22.4 kΩ after 60 min (Figures S10a-b). Irradiation beyond 40 min stabilized resistance at approximately 24.7 kΩ. In contrast, resistance increased to 76.8 kΩ during swelling (3 h), reflecting water accumulation (Figures S10c-d). Optimal irradiation and swelling durations were determined to be 60 min and 3 h, respectively. Impedance during shrinkage and swelling was further investigated (Fig. 4e). Resistance decreased from 43.7 kΩ (step 1) to 22.4 kΩ after solar light treatment (step 2). Swelling increased resistance to 76.8 kΩ (step 3). A second irradiation reduced resistance to 54.1 kΩ (step 4), lower than the decrease in step 2 due to higher water retention from prior cycles. Water accumulation was observed during these processes, driven by slower removal rates compared to intake (Fig. 4f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solar light-driven purification of bacteria-contaminated water based on CD-hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 illustrates the purification of bacteria-contaminated water using solar light-driven CD-hydrogel. The hydrogel was immersed in the contaminated solution to swell and then irradiated with solar light (100 mW/cm²) to release trapped water (Fig. 5a). Resistance measurements (EIS) under varying bacterial densities revealed an increase in resistance corresponding to the accumulation of bacteria (Fig. 5b). Reusability of the CD-hydrogel for bacterial purification was tested over five cycles of immersion and irradiation (Fig. 5c). Initial resistance was 25.2 kΩ, which decreased to 12.5 kΩ upon exposure to solar light. After immersion in the bacterial solution, resistance increased to 27.6 kΩ, reflecting hydrogel swelling and bacterial attachment. Resistance increased progressively with each cycle, reaching 32.6 kΩ after five cycles, indicating cumulative bacterial accumulation on the hydrogel. Confocal imaging and apoptosis assays showed a survivability of 100 % for bacteria before light exposure, with a significant increase in dead bacteria (99.637 %) observed during early and late apoptosis after irradiation (Fig. 5d). This antibacterial activity was attributed to the elevated temperature caused by solar irradiation. Compared to conventional TiO₂-based photocatalysts, which are only active under UV light, the CD-hydrogel exhibited superior antibacterial performance due to its broad light absorption range (UV-Vis-NIR), as shown in (Table S1) [49–54]. SEM analysis (Fig. 5e) of the CD-hydrogel surface revealed significant bacterial accumulation after five purification cycles, highlighting its efficacy in bacterial water purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time monitoring of river water cleansing using solar light-driven CD-hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 depicts the application of CD-hydrogel for real-time purification of river water. The hydrogel was submerged in a sample from the Dalcheon River (Chungju, South Korea) for 1 h at room temperature, followed by irradiation with solar light (100 mW/cm²) (Fig. 6a). Resistance decreased during irradiation as hydrogel shrinkage altered its dimensions. After seven cycles of water absorption, resistance remained constant, showing no further decrease after irradiation due to the hydrogel's ability to trap debris from river water (Fig. 6b). Wireless analysis data were consistent with sourcemeter measurements, showing a resistance decrease after irradiation and an increase after water absorption (Fig. 6c). SEM images confirmed the presence of trapped residue on the hydrogel's surface after the first and seventh absorption cycles (Fig. 6d). The CD-hydrogel was also tested in various wastewater samples with densities of 1.01, 1.05, and 1.10 g/mL (Figure S11a). Impedance increased as wastewater density rose, indicating hydrogel expansion due to water absorption (Figure S11b). Sourcemeter data showed resistance increases from 40.2 kΩ to 70.1, 81.3, and 90.7 kΩ for densities of 1.01, 1.05, and 1.10 g/mL, respectively (Figure S11c). Wireless analysis data corroborated these findings (Figure S11d). Furthermore, the hydrogel's performance in oil/water mixtures at different ratios (1:100, 1:50, 1:20, 1:10, and 1:5) was assessed (Figure S11e). Resistance increased from 40.2 kΩ to 69.1, 80.4, 91.6, 102.6, and 105.4 kΩ as the oil content increased (Figure S11f). This behavior was attributed to hydrophobic interactions between the hydrogel and oil [55]. Wireless analysis confirmed a distinct increase in resistance with higher oil content (Figure S11g).

In this study, a solar light-driven electroconductive CD-hydrogel was successfully fabricated to monitor water desalination through volume transition influenced by temperature changes and visible light irradiation. The thermo-reversibility of the hydrogel matrix enabled volume transitions within temperature ranges of 35 °C–55 °C and 0 °C–25 °C, corresponding to the LCST of PNIPAAm and UCST of PNAGA, respectively. The inclusion of f-cPDA/TiO₂ (CD) adjusted the hydrogel’s affinity for water, transitioning it from hydrophobic to hydrophilic via the photocatalytic effect of TiO₂. This transition reduced the contact angle from 70° to 55°, 58°, and 50° due to the effects of LCST, UCST, and solar irradiation (100 mW/cm²), respectively. The tensile mechanics demonstrated enhanced elongation at break, reaching 169 % and 162 % at 4 °C and 45 °C, respectively. Electrical resistance measurements showed a reduction from 43.7 to 22.4 kΩ after solar light treatment, with an increase to 76.8 kΩ during alternating swelling and irradiation cycles, indicating reduced conductance due to dimensional changes. The purification of bacteria-contaminated water demonstrated that clean water could be effectively treated using absorption–evaporation methods. Additionally, bacterial extermination reached 99.637 % following solar light exposure, attributed to increased temperature during irradiation, highlighting the CD-hydrogel’s antimicrobial efficacy. By leveraging the thermo-responsive volume transition and solar light absorption capabilities of the CD-hydrogel, efficient water purification was achieved. Moreover, real-time monitoring was successfully conducted using a Bluetooth-connected apparatus.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Tae Min Kim obtained her bachelor’s degree from Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation (KNUT) in 2024. She is currently pursuing a master’s degree at Functional Biomaterials Lab, KNUT. She works on stimuli-responsive nanoparticles and hydrogels for various applications, including sensing and drug delivery systems.

Akhmad Irhas Robby received his Ph.D. from Department of Green Bio Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation (KNUT) in 2022. Currently, he is a Research Professor at Chemical Industry Institute, KNUT, Republic of Korea. His research is focused on biomaterials, particularly in utilizing carbon dots/polymer dots and stimuli-responsive hydrogel for biosensing and disease therapeutics, such as bacterial infection, osteoarthritis, aging factors, and cancer.

Gibaek Lee is currently a Professor at Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation (KNUT). He received his Ph.D in Chemical Engineering from Seoul National University in 1997. He was a recipient of Beomseok Paper Award (Korean Institute of Chemical Engineers, 2003). His current research focuses on big data analysis and applications in chemical engineering.

Byung Chan Lee is currently a Professor at Department of Green Bio Engineering and Department of Environmental Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation (KNUT). He received his Ph.D. in Mechanical Engineering from Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST). His research focuses on environmental noise management.

Benny Ryplida graduated with honors from Bandung Institute of Technology (ITB), Indonesia in 2014 with a B.Sc. in Chemistry. In 2022, he received his doctorate from the Department of Green Bio Engineering at Korea National University of Transportation (KNUT). Benny has co-authored over 18 refereed journal articles, 1 patent, and 1 chapter of book, especially in the field of hydrogel and biosensors. He previously worked as a Research Professor at the Chemical Industry Institute, South Korea until 2023. Recently, he worked as researcher in Faculty of Biochemistry and Molecular Medicine in University of Oulu, Finland. His present study is centered on the usage of hydrogel as a biosensing and actuator material, in which will be integrated into microfluidic system.

Sung Young Park received his Ph.D. from the School of Material Chemistry in Japan Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (JAIST) in 2005. He is currently a Professor in the Department of Chemistry and Biological Engineering, Department of Green Bio Engineering and Department of IT & Energy Convergence (BK21 FOUR), Korea National University of Transportation (KNUT). His research group is working at diagnostic nanomedicine, multifunctional hybrid nanomaterials, theranostic nanoparticle and surface chemistry based on new strategies for the imaging and therapy of cancer cells, sensing of different biological molecules and bacterial detection.
